front: Film: knight Antonius Block (Max von Sydow) returns from the Crusades and challenges Death to chess on a rocky beach to prolong his life
Film: ends with Death (hourglass in hand) leading a silhouetted Danse Macabre of Block and companions across a hilltop, watched by Jof
Film: Block goes to confession and inadvertently reveals his chess strategy to Death, who is disguised as a monk in whiteface
Film: Block performs his 'meaningful deed' by distracting Death at the chess board, allowing actor Jof and wife Mia to escape with their infant
Film: Block visits a church where a Danse Macabre fresco is being painted; the final scene recreates it
Film: jester Skat hides in a tree after faking a theatrical suicide; Death saws it down, killing him — a squirrel hops on the stump
Film: thief Raval dies of plague while begging for water; sunlight shines on him the moment he drops dead
Film: a girl is burned at the stake for allegedly sleeping with the Devil; she tells Block the devil is 'everywhere around you'
Film: Block shares a meal of strawberries and milk with Jof and Mia, declaring he will remember 'this moment, these strawberries, this bowl of milk'
Film: a mute servant girl whispers 'It is finished' when Death arrives for Block at the castle
Film: Block announces 'My flesh is afraid, but I am not' when Death first appears on a rocky beach
Film: a traveling troupe's performance is interrupted by a procession of flagellants
Film: adapted from Bergman's own morality play Wood Painting; shot in black-and-white (Bergman's first color film was All These Women)
Film: Death picks the black chess pieces and says 'it becomes me well'; his final lines are 'I have no secrets' and 'I am unknowing'
Film: Smiles of a Summer Night's success at Cannes provided the funding for this film
Film: Death was played by Bengt Ekerot, who later directed the world premiere of Long Day's Journey into Night
